title: Why Your AWS CI/CD Pipeline maybe Slower Than It Should Be (Mine Was Too)
summary: An AWS CI/CD pipeline review reveals that stateless builds without caching waste 6-8 minutes per deployment by unnecessarily reinstalling dependencies and rebuilding layers from scratch. The author demonstrates how enabling CodeBuild caching and optimizing bundled build phases can significantly improve pipeline performance. Proper caching configuration transforms a seemingly functional but inefficient pipeline into a fast, responsive deployment workflow.
content:
It was one of those days where nothing was technically broken… but everything felt off.
Deployments were going through. Pipelines were green. No alarms screaming.
I remember staring at the screen after triggering a simple change. A tiny config tweak. Something that should’ve gone through in a couple of minutes. Instead, I watched my AWS pipeline crawl… step by step… like it had all the time in the world.
For a YAML change.
At the time, I told myself, “Yeah, CI/CD pipelines are just slow sometimes.”
The lie we tell ourselves
If your pipeline works, you stop questioning it.
That’s what I did.
I had a pretty standard setup:
Code pushed → CodePipeline triggers
CodeBuild runs tests + build
Artifacts go to S3
Deploy via CodeDeploy
Nothing exotic. No weird hacks. It looked clean.
But under the surface, it was quietly inefficient in ways I didn’t notice for months.
The moment it clicked
One afternoon, I had to deploy 5 times in a row (😅).
That’s when it hit me:
I was spending more time waiting for my pipeline than actually coding.
And worse… I had accepted it.
Where the time was actually going
I finally sat down and traced a single run end-to-end. Not casually. Properly.
And yeah… it was uncomfortable.
1. CodeBuild was doing way too much
I had bundled everything into one build phase:
install dependencies
run tests
build artifacts
package everything
Seemed efficient, right?
Except… every single run started from scratch.
No caching.
So even if I changed one line, it:
reinstalled node modules
rebuilt layers
redid everything like it had never seen my project before
That alone was eating 6–8 minutes.
What I didn’t realize at the time:
Stateless builds are great… until they’re unnecessarily stateless.
2. I ignored caching because it felt “optional”
AWS makes caching in CodeBuild possible, but not exactly obvious.
I skipped it initially because:
It adds config complexity
Cache invalidation is annoying
“It’s fine for now”
Classic.
When I finally enabled caching for dependencies (n

zh.title: 为什么你的 AWS CI/CD 管道可能比应该的速度慢（我的也是）
zh.summary: AWS CI/CD 管道审查揭示了无状态构建而不进行缓存会在每次部署时浪费 6-8 分钟，因为会不必要地重新安装依赖项并从头重建层。作者演示了如何启用 CodeBuild 缓存和优化捆绑构建阶段可以显著提高管道性能。适当的缓存配置将看似可正常工作但效率低下的管道转变为快速、响应迅速的部署工作流。

title: How to Open Your LTI Tool in a New Tab in 2026 (And What Context to Pass With It)
summary: LTI tools should use iframes for passive content consumption but open in a new tab when delivering complete experiences with their own navigation and state management. The critical challenge is preserving LTI launch context when redirecting outside the iframe, which most tutorials overlook. Making this distinction prevents user confusion and ensures the tool functions as intended.
content:
Most LTI tools default to iframe. The LMS supports it, the docs show it, and it works — until a real user sits down and tries to navigate your product.
Here's what you get from this post: a clear decision rule for iframe vs new tab, what actually breaks when you get it wrong, and the one technical step most tutorials skip entirely.
When your tool loads inside an LMS iframe, the LMS keeps its own sidebar, top nav, and breadcrumbs visible. Your tool has the same. The user now sees two navigation systems on one screen.
They don't file a bug report. They don't understand what's happening technically. They just get confused about which navigation to use — the LMS one or yours — and quietly give up.
I saw this firsthand when a complete course player rendered inside a Moodle iframe. The tool had its own sections, its own progress nav, its own sidebar. Inside the LMS iframe, it looked broken. Not technically broken — visually broken in a way no amount of CSS fixes.
The problem isn't the code. It's that iframe was never designed to contain a complete product experience.
Use iframe when your tool renders something passive — a file, a static page, a read-only embed. The user isn't navigating. They're consuming. Iframe is fine.
Open in a new tab when your tool is a complete experience with its own navigation, flows, or state. Course players, interactive assessments, multi-step tools — anything where the user needs the full screen to understand what they're doing.
A PDF viewer? Iframe works. A course with content, assignments, and quizzes? New tab. A simple content embed? Iframe is fine. An interactive assessment with progress tracking? New tab.
Technically, the redirect itself is simple. In your tool's launch handler, instead of rendering content in the response, you return a page that immediately opens your full application in a new tab.
The part most tutorials skip: when you redirect out of the iframe, you lose the LTI launch context.
The LMS sends you context once — at launc

zh.title: 如何在 2026 年在新标签页中打开你的 LTI 工具（以及要传递什么上下文）
zh.summary: LTI 工具应该使用 iframe 来进行被动内容消费，但在提供具有自己的导航和状态管理的完整体验时应在新标签页中打开。关键的挑战是在重定向到 iframe 外部时保持 LTI 启动上下文，而大多数教程都忽视了这一点。做出这种区分可防止用户困惑并确保工具按预期工作。

title: Decoded — a simple way to actually understand DSA
summary: Decoded is a learning platform that shifts DSA education from memorization toward understanding by showing step-by-step problem approaches and pattern recognition. It includes interactive dry runs tracking variable and pointer changes, complexity analysis with reasoning, and a patterns page grouping problems by common algorithmic techniques. This approach helps learners recognize problem types rather than merely remembering isolated solutions.
content:
👉https://decoded-app.vercel.app/
When you're learning DSA, there’s a point where things start feeling repetitive.
You solve problems.
But a few days later, the same type of problem feels new again.
That usually happens because you're remembering solutions, not the thinking behind them.
Decoded is a small web app built to fix that.
The goal is pretty simple:
Make it easier to understand how to approach problems — not just how to code the solution.
Instead of dumping answers, it focuses on:
breaking problems down
showing how the solution evolves
helping you recognize patterns
Each problem comes with:
a clear explanation
a structured approach
a step-by-step dry run
The dry run is the important part.
You can actually follow:
how variables change
how pointers move
how the solution builds up
So you can see everything happen instead of imagining it.
Every solution includes:
time complexity
space complexity
But instead of just stating Big-O, the focus is on why it’s that value.
One of the most useful sections is the Patterns page.
A lot of DSA problems aren’t unique — they’re variations of a few common ideas.
This page groups problems based on patterns like:
two pointers
sliding window
prefix sum
Each pattern explains:
how it works
when to use it
what kind of problems it fits
Over time, you stop thinking “I’ve seen this before”
this looks like a sliding window problem.”
That’s a big difference.
The Data Structures page focuses on fundamentals.
It covers things like:
arrays
linked lists
stacks
queues
The idea isn’t to go deep into theory, but to keep it practical.
Each structure is explained in terms of:
what it does
how it behaves
So instead of just knowing definitions, you start understanding when to use what.
beginners starting DSA
people preparing for interviews

zh.title: Decoded — 真正理解 DSA 的简单方式
zh.summary: Decoded 是一个学习平台，通过展示逐步的问题方法和模式识别，将 DSA 教育从死记硬背转向理解。它包括跟踪变量和指针变化的交互式干式运行、带有推理的复杂度分析，以及按常见算法技术对问题进行分组的模式页面。这种方法帮助学习者识别问题类型，而不是仅仅记住孤立的解决方案。

title: The "Senior Developer" Trap: Why Being the Best Coder is Actually Holding You Back-(An inspirational content)
summary: True senior development transcends technical prowess; it's about solving business problems and multiplying team effectiveness rather than being the only person who can fix a system. Over-focusing on code elegance while ignoring business outcomes misaligns with actual developer value. Senior developers should prioritize simplifying systems, mentoring others, and enabling the business over perfectionistic coding.
content:
I spent the first five years of my career convinced that my value was tied to how many obscure libraries I knew and how "clean" my PRs were. I wanted to be the person who could fix the bug that nobody else could touch.
The "Clean Code" Obsession vs. Business Reality
We’ve all seen it: A developer spends three days refactoring a module to make it "elegant" while the client’s main pain point—say, a massive electricity bill or a broken supply chain—remains unsolved.
The Reality: Business owners don't care if you used a Factory Pattern or a simple If-Else. They care about the outcome. A Senior Dev knows when to write "quick and dirty" code to test a business hypothesis and when to build a robust architecture.
Takeaway: Stop coding for other developers. Start coding for the person paying the bill.
We are no longer just "Coders"—We are Energy Savers and Efficiency Experts
If you’re working on a project like a Solar Management System, your code isn't just logic; it’s literally saving someone money and reducing carbon footprints.
When you shift your mindset from "I’m building an API" to "I’m helping a factory stay profitable during a heatwave," your decisions change. You become more careful with data precision. You think about offline-first capabilities for remote areas.
The "Hero" Complex is a Bottleneck
If you are the only one who can fix the system, you aren't a Senior; you’re a single point of failure.
True seniority is about multiplication.
Can you explain a complex cloud migration to a non-technical CEO?
Can you write documentation so clear that a junior doesn't need to ping you on Slack at 9 PM?
Can you simplify a process so much that the code almost feels "boring"?
Technical Debt is a Financial Decision, not just a Coding One
In the world of Digital Transformation, we talk a lot about "Legacy Systems." Most devs hate them. But those legacy systems are often the ones currently generating the revenue that pays our salaries.
Instead of complaining about old code, learn

zh.title: "高级开发者"陷阱：为什么成为最好的编码员实际上在阻碍你的进步
zh.summary: 真正的高级开发不只是技术实力；它关乎解决业务问题和提升团队效能，而不是成为唯一能修复系统的人。过度关注代码优雅而忽视业务成果与实际开发者价值不符。高级开发者应该优先考虑简化系统、指导他人和支持业务，而不是过度追求代码完美。

title: How Economic Apathy and Political Greed are Pushing the Philippines to the Brink
summary: As of March 2026, the Philippines faces a severe economic crisis marked by nationwide strikes protesting fuel prices and inflation, met with government inaction on tax relief or energy emergency declarations. The administration's simultaneous swift dissolution of the infrastructure watchdog reveals selective capacity to act, prioritizing political protection over public welfare. The crisis has sparked calls for expanded mass demonstrations as workers realize only collective resistance will force government response.
content:
As of late March 2026, the streets of Manila are no longer just corridors for commerce; they have become the front lines of a desperate struggle for survival. On March 20, the Philippine transport sector launched a massive two-day nationwide strike, a visceral reaction to the crushing weight of skyrocketing fuel prices and runaway inflation that the Marcos Jr. administration has effectively ignored. Despite the clear economic distress paralyzing the working class, the government’s response has been one of chilling indifference. By flatly refusing to declare a national energy emergency or implement a much-needed cut in fuel taxes, the administration has signaled to millions of Filipinos that their survival is not a priority. This calculated inaction, framed as "fiscal responsibility," is a death sentence for grassroots laborers already pushed to the edge. The current economic crisis is not merely a global fluctuation; it is a domestic disaster exacerbated by a government that is "incapable" of providing relief to its citizens while remaining hyper-active in protecting its own political interests. This systemic failure has fueled a call for expanded strikes and mass demonstrations, as the public realizes that only collective resistance can force a response from a regime that has turned its back on the people’s livelihood.
The irony of the government's "helplessness" regarding fuel costs is laid bare by its simultaneous and highly efficient maneuvers to protect its inner circle. On March 15, the Malacañang Palace moved with lightning speed to dissolve the Independent Commission on Infrastructure (ICI), the primary watchdog for auditing massive public works projects. This move, seen as a blatant attempt to shield corrupt allies and halt any meaningful anti-graft investigations since the start of 2026, proves that the administration does have the power to act—it simply chooses to use that power for self-preservation rather than public welfare. While the transport sector

zh.title: 经济冷漠和政治贪婪如何将菲律宾推向悬崖边缘
zh.summary: 截至2026年3月，菲律宾面临严重经济危机，全国爆发抗议燃料价格和通货膨胀的罢工，但政府对税收减免或能源紧急宣言毫无行动。政府同时迅速解散基础设施监督机构，显示其选择性行动能力，优先保护政治利益而非公众福利。危机引发了扩大大规模示威的呼声，因为工人们意识到只有集体抵抗才能迫使政府做出回应。

title: SubAgents: Langchain Agents as Tools with Langfuse using AWS Bedrock Nova 🤖
summary: Subagents are specialized AI agents handling specific tasks within larger systems; treating them as tools—with defined input/output schemas—improves modularity, scalability, and debugging isolation. Unlike autonomous subagents with shared state and turn-taking logic, subagent-as-tools are black boxes callable by a main agent with zero autonomy and explicit invocation only. This pattern suits systems requiring clear control flow and parameter integrity.
content:
AI agents are powerful, but they become truly effective when they’re broken down into smaller, specialized parts. This post introduces the idea of subagents, how they work, why they matter, and how treating them like tools can make your systems more modular, scalable, and easier to debug.
What are SubAgents?
Subagents are smaller, specialized AI agents that handle specific tasks (e.g., search, coding, data analysis) within a larger agent system.
The main agent delegates work to these subagents, letting each one focus on what it does best.
Using subagents as tools means treating each subagent like a callable function with a defined input/output schema.
They improve accuracy because each subagent focuses on a narrow responsibility instead of trying to do everything.
They enable scalability, since you can add, remove, or upgrade individual subagents without changing the whole system.
They also make debugging and monitoring easier by isolating where errors or failures happen.
Differences SubAgents vs SubAgents-As-Tools
SubAgents are often treated as independent entities that share a common workspace.
Communication: They might talk to each other or a manager agent via a shared state. With LangGraph, we can define shared state between agents.
Autonomy: The manager says, "Research this startup," and the sub-agents decide when and how to report back. They may even initiate conversations with one another without the manager's direct intervention.
Flow: Usually managed by a Graph or State Machine where the "turn-taking" is governed by logic.
SubAgent-As-Tool: The SubAgent is wrapped inside a tool definition.
Communication: The main agent sees the sub-agent as a black box. It passes an input and waits for a specific output.
Autonomy: The SubAgent has zero autonomy. It only speaks when spoken to. It cannot volunteer information; it is explicitly called.
Flow: The main agent’s execution pauses until the tool (subAgent) returns its finding.
Today, in this post, we're exploring th

zh.title: SubAgents：使用AWS Bedrock Nova通过Langfuse将Langchain Agents作为工具 🤖
zh.summary: SubAgents是在更大系统中处理特定任务的专门AI代理；将它们视为工具——具有明确的输入/输出模式——改进了模块性、可扩展性和调试隔离。与具有共享状态和轮流逻辑的自主子代理不同，子代理即工具是主代理可调用的黑盒，零自主性和仅显式调用。这种模式适合需要清晰控制流和参数完整性的系统。

title: Your MCP Server Has No Audit Trail — A Security Checklist
summary: MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers ship without request signing, audit logging, or identity verification, creating risk for parameter tampering, replay attacks, and impersonation—as demonstrated by recent production incidents at Amazon Kiro, Replit, and Supabase. The MCP spec provides no cryptographic proof of what happened, blocking incident response and compliance audits. The article provides a practical security checklist to add these critical controls.
content:
Your MCP Server Has No Audit Trail — A Security Checklist


Last month, an AI agent mass-deleted a production environment. The team spent 3 days piecing together what happened — stderr logs, partial timestamps, no proof of which agent or what parameters. No audit trail.
This isn't rare. Amazon Kiro deleted a prod environment. Replit's agent dropped a live database. Supabase MCP leaked tokens via prompt injection. In every case: zero cryptographic evidence of what happened.
MCP is becoming the standard for agent-tool communication. Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, and dozens of tools use it. But the MCP spec ships with:
❌ No request signing
❌ No audit log
❌ No caller identity verification
❌ No replay protection
❌ No parameter integrity checks
Your MCP server accepts any request from any process, trusts it completely, and keeps no verifiable record. Here's a practical checklist to fix that.
Before the checklist, understand what you're defending against:



Attack
How it works
Impact




Parameter tampering
Agent sends create_issue("fix bug"), something in the pipeline changes it to delete_repo("production")

Data loss


Replay
Legitimate deploy_to_prod captured and replayed 50 times
Repeated side effects


Impersonation
Rogue process sends requests claiming to be your trusted agent
Unauthorized actions


Cross-server forwarding
Request intended for staging gets forwarded to production
Wrong environment


Log tampering
Text logs edited after an incident to cover tracks
No incident response


Compliance gap
SOC 2 / HIPAA / GDPR require audit trails; "the AI did it" is not sufficient
Regulatory risk



If your MCP server uses HTTP (SSE or Streamable HTTP), always terminate TLS. This protects data in transit but does not protect against:
Compromised clients sending bad requests
Replay attacks (TLS protects the pipe, not the message)
Log tampering after the fact
For stdio transports (most local MCP servers), TLS doesn't apply — the attack surface is different (any local proc

zh.title: 你的 MCP 服务器没有审计跟踪——安全检查清单
zh.summary: MCP（模型上下文协议）服务器在发布时缺少请求签名、审计日志或身份验证，这在参数篡改、重放攻击和冒充方面带来风险——亚马逊 Kiro、Replit 和 Supabase 的最近生产事故证明了这一点。MCP 规范没有提供发生了什么的密码学证明，阻碍了事件响应和合规性审计。该文章提供了一个实用的安全检查清单来添加这些关键控制措施。

title: URL Encoding Explained: What Every Developer Needs to Know (2026)
summary: URL encoding converts special characters into percent-encoded format (spaces become %20, & becomes %26) to safely transmit data in URLs, following RFC 3986. Proper encoding is essential for preventing API parameter confusion, defending against injection attacks, and preserving data integrity across proxies and analytics systems. Multi-byte UTF-8 characters like emoji require encoding as sequences of %XX pairs.
content:
Ever wondered why your URLs have %20 instead of spaces, or why your API breaks when a parameter contains an &? That's URL encoding at work — and understanding it will save you hours of debugging.
URL encoding (also called percent encoding) converts characters into a format safe for URLs. Since URLs can only contain a limited set of ASCII characters, anything outside that set gets replaced with % followed by two hex digits.
For example:
Space → %20


& → %26


= → %3D


# → %23


@ → %40


+ → %2B



This is defined in RFC 3986 and is fundamental to how the web works.
Consider searching for "rock & roll":
❌ Bad:  https://api.example.com/search?q=rock & roll
✅ Good: https://api.example.com/search?q=rock%20%26%20roll

Without encoding, the & is interpreted as a parameter separator, breaking your query into two malformed parameters.
Unencoded URLs are a vector for injection attacks (XSS, SQL injection). Encoding neutralizes special characters by converting them to harmless percent-encoded representations.
Non-ASCII characters (Chinese, Arabic, emoji) must be encoded using their UTF-8 byte sequences:
café → caf%C3%A9
日本 → %E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC
🚀  → %F0%9F%9A%80

URLs pass through proxies, load balancers, logging systems, and analytics tools. Encoding ensures nothing gets corrupted along the way.
The algorithm is simple:
Take the character's UTF-8 byte value

Write % + two hex digits for each byte
ASCII characters (1 byte):



Character
Decimal
Hex
Encoded




Space
32
20
%20


!
33
21
%21


#
35
23
%23


&
38
26
%26


+
43
2B
%2B


/
47
2F
%2F


=
61
3D
%3D


?
63
3F
%3F


@
64
40
%40



Multi-byte characters produce multiple %XX sequences:
é (2 bytes UTF-8) → %C3%A9
中 (3 bytes UTF-8) → %E4%B8%AD
🎉 (4 bytes UTF-8) → %F0%9F%8E%89

A-Z  a-z  0-9  -  .  _  ~

These are always safe in any URL position.
:  /  ?  #  [  ]  @  !  $  &  '  (  )  *  +  ,  ;  =

These have special meaning in URLs. Encode them when they appear as data (parameter values), not when they serve their str

zh.title: URL 编码解释：每个开发者都需要了解的知识（2026）
zh.summary: URL 编码将特殊字符转换为百分号编码格式（空格变为 %20，& 变为 %26），以安全地在 URL 中传输数据，遵循 RFC 3986。正确的编码对于防止 API 参数混淆、防御注入攻击和跨代理及分析系统保留数据完整性至关重要。多字节 UTF-8 字符（如表情符号）需要编码为 %XX 对的序列。

title: i18next in Production: Managing Translations at Scale
summary: Production-scale translation management requires moving from in-repo JSON files to runtime CDN delivery using i18next-http-backend or managed services, eliminating merge conflicts and deploy blockage. This pattern decouples translation updates from code deployment and removes the need for translators to have git access, enabling non-technical contributors to manage content independently. Different teams can own different namespaces without visibility into the complete translation picture.

zh.title: i18next 在生产环境中的应用：大规模翻译管理
zh.summary: 生产规模的翻译管理需要从仓库内的 JSON 文件转移到使用 i18next-http-backend 或托管服务的运行时 CDN 交付，消除合并冲突和部署阻碍。这种模式将翻译更新与代码部署解耦，并消除了翻译人员拥有 git 访问权限的需要，使非技术贡献者能够独立管理内容。不同的团队可以拥有不同的命名空间，而无需查看完整的翻译内容。

title: Don't Let Bad Networks Kill Your App: Building Offline-First in Flutter & Laravel 📱
summary: Offline-first architecture treats the local on-device database as the source of truth, with background synchronization handling eventual consistency, making applications resilient to network drops—critical for field-based services like AgriTech. Flutter (SQLite) manages local data with sync flags while Laravel backends reconcile changes, ensuring responsive UX regardless of connectivity. This pattern prioritizes user experience in low-bandwidth environments over always-online assumptions.

zh.title: 别让糟糕的网络毁掉你的应用：在 Flutter 和 Laravel 中构建离线优先架构 📱
zh.summary: 离线优先架构将本地设备数据库视为真实数据源，通过后台同步处理最终一致性，使应用程序能够抵御网络中断——这对于 AgriTech 等基于现场的服务至关重要。Flutter (SQLite) 通过同步标志管理本地数据，而 Laravel 后端协调更改，确保无论连接状况如何都能提供响应式用户体验。这种模式优先考虑低带宽环境中的用户体验，而非始终在线的假设。

title: Getting NIGHT Tokens: Exchanges, Bridging & Wallet Funding on Mainnet
summary: NIGHT tokens power the Midnight privacy-focused blockchain; they can be acquired through exchanges like MEXC Global and Gate.io or decentralized trading within the ecosystem. The guide provides step-by-step account creation, KYC verification, and wallet setup instructions for mainnet participation. Users should monitor official Midnight channels for evolving exchange listings and ecosystem updates.
content:
Getting NIGHT Tokens: Exchanges, Bridging & Wallet Funding on Mainnet



A practical guide to acquiring NIGHT tokens and getting your wallet ready for the Midnight ecosystem.
Midnight is a privacy-focused blockchain platform built on Cardano, designed for developers who want to build decentralized applications (DApps) with programmable confidentiality controls. To interact with the Midnight network — whether you're deploying smart contracts, testing DApps, or simply participating in the ecosystem — you'll need NIGHT tokens.
This tutorial walks you through every step of acquiring NIGHT tokens, from choosing the right exchange to setting up a compatible wallet. We'll cover the current state of exchanges, bridging options, and what's on the horizon.
Before diving in, make sure you have:
A basic understanding of cryptocurrency wallets and exchanges
A device with internet access
An email address for exchange registration
As of April 2026, NIGHT tokens are available on several exchanges. Here's a breakdown:
MEXC Global is currently one of the primary exchanges where NIGHT is actively traded. To get started:
Create an account at MEXC

Complete KYC verification (required for withdrawals)
Deposit USDT or another supported trading pair
Search for the NIGHT/USDT trading pair
Place a market or limit order
Gate.io also supports NIGHT trading:
Register at Gate.io

Complete identity verification
Fund your account with USDT, ETH, or BTC
Navigate to the NIGHT trading pair
Execute your trade
If you prefer decentralized trading, NIGHT may also be available on DEXs within the Midnight ecosystem. These typically require:
A Web3 wallet (like Lace or a compatible Cardano wallet)
Some base tokens for gas fees
Familiarity with liquidity pools and slippage settings
Pro tip: Always check the official Midnight channels for the latest exchange listings. The ecosystem is evolving rapidly, and new listings happen regularly.
Once you have NIGHT tokens, you need somewhere to store them. Midnight is

zh.title: 获取 NIGHT 代币：交易所、跨链桥接和 Mainnet 钱包充值
zh.summary: NIGHT 代币为隐私焦点区块链 Midnight 提供动力；可通过 MEXC Global 和 Gate.io 等交易所或生态系统内的去中心化交易获得。本指南提供逐步的账户创建、KYC 验证和钱包设置说明，用于 Mainnet 参与。用户应监控 Midnight 官方渠道，了解交易所列表和生态系统更新的最新情况。

title: Kaufland.SellerApi Unleashed: Build Marketplace Integrations in Minutes with .NET 8
summary: Kaufland.SellerApi is an open-source .NET 8 SDK that abstracts marketplace API complexity by handling HMAC-SHA256 authentication, rate limiting, and HTTP resilience automatically. The strongly-typed wrapper eliminates boilerplate for signature computation and timestamp management, enabling developers to build robust marketplace integrations in minutes rather than weeks. It integrates seamlessly with .NET Dependency Injection, reducing ceremony to focus on business logic.
content:
TL;DR: Kaufland.SellerApi is a modern, open-source C# SDK built for .NET 8 that wraps all 24 Kaufland Marketplace Seller API endpoints. It handles HMAC-SHA256 request signing automatically, ships with built-in HTTP resilience, and plugs directly into the .NET Dependency Injection system — so you can skip the plumbing and go straight to building.
In the competitive world of e-commerce, speed to market is everything. If you're a .NET developer tasked with integrating a seller's catalog, inventory, or orders with Kaufland — one of Europe's leading online marketplaces — you've likely stared at their OpenAPI documentation and wondered about the best way to handle HMAC signatures, rate limits, and DTO mapping.
Enter the Kaufland Seller API SDK for .NET.
In this post, we're going to dive deep into how you can leverage this modern, community-driven SDK to build a robust integration in minutes rather than weeks. Whether you're managing thousands of SKUs or automating order fulfillment, this guide will get you up and running with best practices firmly in mind.
If you've ever tried to integrate a marketplace API from scratch, you know the drill. Before you can write a single line of business logic, you're already deep in the weeds: constructing HMAC signatures, managing Unix timestamps, handling HTTP 429 rate-limit responses, wiring up retries, and massaging raw JSON into usable objects.
The Kaufland Marketplace Seller API v2 is no different. It's a capable, well-documented API — but integrating it directly in a .NET project means dealing with all of that ceremony yourself. Every request must carry a Shop-Signature header computed via HMAC-SHA256, a matching Shop-Timestamp, and a Shop-Client-Key. Get any of it wrong and you get a 401. Get the timestamp stale and you get another 401.
That's exactly the problem the Kaufland.SellerApi SDK solves. It's a community-driven, open-source, strongly-typed C# wrapper built natively for .NET 8. It handles all of the authentication, resili

zh.title: Kaufland.SellerApi 发布：使用 .NET 8 在数分钟内构建市场集成
zh.summary: Kaufland.SellerApi 是一个开源 .NET 8 SDK，通过自动处理 HMAC-SHA256 身份验证、速率限制和 HTTP 弹性来抽象市场 API 的复杂性。强类型包装器消除了签名计算和时间戳管理的样板代码，使开发人员能够在数分钟而不是数周内构建健壮的市场集成。它与 .NET 依赖注入无缝集成，减少了仪式化，使开发者能专注于业务逻辑。

title: ⚔️ Terraform Workspaces vs Environments — Part 7
summary: Guide comparing Terraform workspaces (single config with multiple state files) against environment-based structures (separate folders) for infrastructure management. Workspaces simplify code reuse but lack clarity for teams, while environment structures scale better for production systems. Recommends environment-based approach for production and team-based deployments.
content:
So far in this series, we’ve:
Deployed infrastructure with Terraform
Used variables and outputs
Built reusable modules
Implemented remote backend (S3 + DynamoDB)
Structured projects using dev / staging / prod
Now, there’s an important question 👇
👉 Should you use Terraform Workspaces instead of environments?
Let’s break it down.
In this guide:
What Terraform workspaces are
How they work
Differences between workspaces and environments
When to use each approach
A workspace is:
👉 A way to manage multiple state files using the same Terraform configuration
By default, Terraform uses:
terraform workspace list

Output:
default

terraform workspace new dev
terraform workspace new prod

Switch between them:
terraform workspace select dev

👉 Each workspace represents a separate state.
Terraform stores state per workspace:
terraform.tfstate.d/
  dev/
  prod/

👉 Same code
resource "aws_instance" "example" {
  instance_type = terraform.workspace == "prod" ? "t3.medium" : "t2.micro"
}

👉 Behavior changes based on workspace.
Workspaces allow you to:
Avoid duplicating code
Manage multiple environments quickly
Workspaces seem simple… but in real-world projects 👇
❌ Not very clear for teams
From Part 6:
environments/
  dev/
  staging/
  prod/

👉 Each environment has:
Separate folder
Separate backend
Clear structure
Let’s compare 👇
Workspaces → simple but limited
Environments → structured and scalable
👉 Workspaces are useful for:
Quick testing
Personal projects
👉 Environments are better for:
Production systems
Team collaboration
Long-term infrastructure
👉 Use environment-based structure for real-world projects
👉 Use workspaces only for simple use cases
Do not rely only on workspaces for:
Production infrastructure
Team-based deployments
👉 It increases risk.
How Terraform workspaces work
Their advantages and limitations
Why environments are preferred in production
Terraform gives you multiple ways to solve a problem.
👉 But not all approaches scale.
Choosing the right struct

zh.title: ⚔️ Terraform 工作区 vs 环境 — 第7部分
zh.summary: 指南对比 Terraform 工作区（单个配置文件配合多个状态文件）与基于环境的结构（分离的文件夹）用于基础设施管理。工作区简化代码复用但对团队缺乏清晰度，而环境结构对生产系统的可扩展性更好。建议在生产环境和基于团队的部署中采用基于环境的方法。

title: The Uncomfortable Truth About Loyalty At Your First Job 💸
summary: Early-career software engineers face stagnant salary growth through loyalty, with typical annual raises capped at 3-5%, while external job changes offer 20% compensation increases. The mismatch stems from companies budgeting conservatively for internal raises but paying market rate for new hires. Understanding this structural reality helps early-career engineers navigate compensation strategies without attributing gaps to manager unfairness.
content:
You know that feeling when you’ve been at a company for two years, you’re doing great work, and your raise comes in at… 4%?
Meanwhile, your college friend who jumped ship after 14 months just got a 20% bump in total comp.
That stings. And it teaches you something important early: loyalty is rarely rewarded in your first few years in tech.
I learned this the hard way. Let’s break it down 👇
When I started my career as a software engineer at Netskope, I thought the playbook was simple. 
Work hard, be reliable, and the company will take care of you. 
That’s what everyone tells us growing up. 
And look, Netskope was a great experience. I learned a ton. But after about a year, I made the move to Amazon and saw something eye-opening: the biggest jump in my compensation didn’t come from being at the same place for another year. It came from changing jobs. 
Not just in pay. But also in the kind of problems I got to solve.
Staying put would have been comfortable, but comfort never leads to growth.
Subscribe to get posts like this in your inbox
I have come to understand that this isn’t about your manager being unfair. It’s just a structure everyone follows.

Most companies budget 3-5% for annual raises across the board. That’s the pool. Your manager might fight to get you 7%, and that’s considered exceptional internally. 
But when a company is hiring externally? They’re paying market rate. And market rate moves a lot faster than 3-5% a year, especially in your first decade in tech when your skills are compounding quickly.
So you end up in this weird situation where the new hire sitting next to you, doing the same job, might be making $20-30K more. 
Simply because they negotiated a fresh offer while you’ve been collecting incremental raises.
Companies know this. 
But they also know that most people won’t leave. 
Inertia is powerful. You like your team, you’re mid-project, you don’t want to interview. They’re betting on that.
I’m not saying quit every two years like clockwork.

zh.title: 关于你第一份工作中的忠诚的不舒适真相 💸
zh.summary: 早期职业软件工程师因忠诚面临工资增长停滞，年度加薪上限通常为3-5%，而外部工作变化提供20%的薪酬增长。这种差异源于公司对内部加薪的保守预算，但对新员工支付市场价格。理解这一结构性现实帮助早期职业工程师制定薪酬策略，无需将差异归咎于经理的不公平。

title: Cx Dev Log — 2026-04-12
summary: The Cx compiler project added support for infinite loops, break, and continue statements via a new LoopContext structure that properly manages control flow graphs and SSA bindings. The changes fix critical bugs in nested control flow handling and redirect existing tests toward improved language features. These substantial modifications await integration into the main branch after a three-day development period.
content:
Two notable changes hit submain today, breaking a three-day streak of quiet. The Phase 10 IR lowering now covers infinite loop, break, and continue, and a fresh process document defines the agent's operating approach. While both await integration into main, these updates provide key insights into language design and team workflow.
The real highlight is the bcd49f2 commit. Modifications in src/ir/lower.rs, with 215 lines added and 16 removed, introduce a LoopContext structure that guides IR transformations to support infinite loops and related constructs like break and continue.
To be specific:
lower_loop Function: This creates a control flow graph (CFG) for infinite loops by setting up an unconditional jump into the loop's header, executing the loop body, and terminating at an exit block. This ensures the block parameters align with the loop's binding set.
lower_while Updates: The exit block now correctly uses block parameters, fixing a prior bug. The new approach properly routes else_args in the header's Branch terminator, preserving loop-carried SSA through early exit paths.
SemanticStmt::Break and Continue: Both terms gather current SSA values for bindings, emitting a Jump terminator to respective targets (exit_id or header_id), effectively closing the block.
Adjusting lower_stmt: Now incorporates loop_ctx to handle nested control flow scenarios accurately. It fixes a critical issue where break within nested if statements didn’t resolve to the loop’s exit.
Error Handling: Constructs outside loops return UnsupportedSemanticConstruct, and missing SSA bindings throw InternalInvariantViolation.
Refactoring Tests: Existing tests got redirected from Loop to For, setting stage for upcoming improvements.
The structured break/continue commitment aligns with the roadmap, excluding labeled breaks for now—a post-0.1 concern.
Commit 03d2da6 introduces substantial documentation with AGENT_OPERATING_DOCTRINE.md, mapping out a six-phase workflow: Recon, Design Lock, Predicted Fa

zh.title: Cx 开发日志 — 2026-04-12
zh.summary: Cx 编译器项目通过新的 LoopContext 结构添加了对无限循环、break 和 continue 语句的支持，该结构正确管理控制流图和 SSA 绑定。这些更改修复了嵌套控制流处理中的关键错误，并将现有测试重定向向改进的语言功能。这些重大修改在三天开发期后等待集成到主分支中。

title: 6 MCP Servers for 3D & AR Development — What AI Can Build Now
summary: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open Anthropic standard enabling AI assistants to call specialized external tools for domain-specific code generation and validation. The author developed six MCP servers for 3D and AR development (SceneView, automotive, healthcare, gaming) that enable AI to generate compilable code with proper APIs and error handling. This approach bridges the gap between AI capabilities and production-ready implementation across specialized domains.
content:
What if you could ask Claude "build me a car configurator" and get compilable Android code in 10 seconds?
MCP (Model Context Protocol) makes this real. An MCP server gives AI assistants access to specialized tools — code generators, validators, domain knowledge — so they produce working code instead of generic suggestions.
I built 6 MCP servers for 3D and AR development. Here's what each one does and how to use them.
MCP is an open protocol (by Anthropic) that lets AI assistants call external tools. Think of it as plugins, but standardized — one server works with Claude, Cursor, Windsurf, and any MCP-compatible client.
An MCP server provides:
Tools: functions the AI can call (generate code, validate, search)
Resources: data the AI can read (API docs, samples)
Prompts: pre-built workflows (code review, scaffolding)
The flagship. Covers all SceneView features across Android, iOS, and Web.
npx sceneview-mcp

Key tools:
generate_scene — complete scene code from a description
validate_code — catches threading bugs, null handling, wrong APIs
search_models — searches Sketchfab for free 3D models
get_sample — returns working sample code for any feature
Example prompt: "Create a 3D product viewer with orbit camera and environment lighting"
npx automotive-3d-mcp

Specialized for automotive UX: car configurators, HUD overlays, 3D dashboards, AR showrooms, parts catalogs. Knows ADAS display patterns, Tier-1 integration requirements, and automotive UX standards.
Example prompt: "Build a car color configurator with paint + wheel options"
npx healthcare-3d-mcp

Anatomy viewers, DICOM slice rendering, molecular structures, surgical planning tools, dental scanning visualization. Includes HIPAA-compliant patterns and medical imaging standards.
Example prompt: "Create an interactive anatomy model with organ highlighting"
npx gaming-3d-mcp

Character controllers, terrain generation, particle systems, physics interactions, inventory UI, NPC behaviors. Covers casual games, not AAA — opti

zh.title: 6个用于3D和AR开发的MCP服务器——AI现在能构建什么
zh.summary: 模型上下文协议(MCP)是Anthropic开发的开放标准,使AI助手能够调用专门的外部工具进行特定领域的代码生成和验证。作者为3D和AR开发(SceneView、汽车、医疗、游戏)开发了6个MCP服务器,使AI能够生成具有适当API和错误处理的可编译代码。这种方法缩小了AI能力与专业领域生产就绪实现之间的差距。

title: TypeScript Utility Types That Actually Save Time in Production SaaS Code
summary: TypeScript utility types like Partial<T>, Pick<T, K>, Omit<T, K>, and Record<K, V> are essential patterns in production SaaS code for type-safe operations across database layers, APIs, and data serialization. Practical applications include patch operations with Omit, safe client data with Pick, and Stripe integrations where explicit types prevent security leaks and API mistakes. Combining these utilities creates self-documenting contracts between system components.

zh.title: 在生产SaaS代码中确实节省时间的TypeScript实用类型
zh.summary: TypeScript实用类型如Partial<T>、Pick<T, K>、Omit<T, K>和Record<K, V>是生产SaaS代码中必不可少的模式,用于数据库层、API和数据序列化的类型安全操作。实际应用包括使用Omit进行补丁操作、使用Pick进行安全客户端数据以及Stripe集成,其中显式类型可防止安全泄漏和API错误。结合这些实用程序可创建系统组件之间的自文档化契约。

title: The Only Compose-Native 3D & AR Library for Android
summary: SceneView is a Jetpack Compose library enabling declarative 3D and AR development on Android, filling the gap left by deprecated Sceneform and eliminating the need for heavy alternatives like Unity. Built on Google Filament engine with 26+ composable node types (ModelNode, SphereNode, VideoNode), it treats 3D objects as composables with state management matching standard Compose patterns. The library enables complex AR interactions without callback-heavy code.
content:
You're building an Android app with Jetpack Compose. You need 3D. Maybe a product viewer, a model configurator, or AR furniture placement.
You search. You ask ChatGPT. You try Copilot. And you get: Unity (50MB+, no Compose), raw Filament (1000 lines to render a cube), or Sceneform (deprecated since 2021).
There's a gap. Android has no built-in 3D composable. Until now.
SceneView treats 3D the same way Compose treats UI: declaratively. SceneView { } is a composable like Column { } or LazyList { }. Nodes are composables. State is Kotlin state.
implementation("io.github.sceneview:sceneview:4.0.0")

@Composable
fun ProductViewer() {
    val engine = rememberEngine()
    val modelLoader = rememberModelLoader(engine)

    SceneView(
        modifier = Modifier.fillMaxSize(),
        engine = engine,
        modelLoader = modelLoader,
        cameraManipulator = rememberCameraManipulator()
    ) {
        rememberModelInstance(modelLoader, "models/sneaker.glb")?.let {
            ModelNode(modelInstance = it, scaleToUnits = 1.0f, autoAnimate = true)
        }
    }
}

Place sneaker.glb in src/main/assets/models/. The model loads asynchronously, rememberModelInstance returns null while loading, then triggers recomposition. No callbacks, no lifecycle — just state.
SceneView includes 26+ composable node types:
ModelNode — GLB/glTF models with animation
CubeNode, SphereNode, CylinderNode — primitive geometry
ImageNode — textured quads
VideoNode — video textures on 3D surfaces
LightNode — point, spot, directional lights
TextNode — 3D text billboards
ViewNode — any Compose UI in 3D space
All powered by Google Filament — the same PBR engine used in Google Maps and Android Auto.
implementation("io.github.sceneview:arsceneview:4.0.0")

@Composable
fun ARFurniturePlacement() {
    val engine = rememberEngine()
    val modelLoader = rememberModelLoader(engine)
    val anchors = remember { mutableStateListOf<Anchor>() }
    val chair = rememberModelInstance(modelLoader, "models/chair.

zh.title: Android上唯一的原生Compose 3D和AR库
zh.summary: SceneView是一个Jetpack Compose库,在Android上实现声明式3D和AR开发,填补了已弃用Sceneform留下的空白,无需使用Unity等重型替代方案。它基于Google Filament引擎构建,具有26+可组合节点类型(ModelNode、SphereNode、VideoNode),将3D对象视为具有与标准Compose模式相匹配的状态管理的可组合。该库可实现复杂的AR交互,而无需冗长的回调代码。

title: RGAA 4.1: France's Web Accessibility Standard Explained for Non-French Teams
summary: RGAA 4.1 is France's legally mandated web accessibility standard based on WCAG 2.1 but significantly stricter and prescriptive, becoming enforceable on private sector websites from June 2025. Unlike WCAG's descriptive guidelines, RGAA specifies exact tools and verification methods, creating compliance requirements distinct from international standards. Teams managing French websites must address RGAA criteria beyond standard WCAG AA compliance to avoid legal exposure.
content:
RGAA 4.1: France's Web Accessibility Standard Explained for Non-French Teams



Meta: RGAA is France's legally mandated accessibility framework (106 criteria). If your team manages French websites or serves French users, you must understand it. Here's what RGAA is, how it differs from WCAG, who's affected post-June 2025, and the 5 most commonly failed criteria with fixes.
Your team manages websites in France. Or you have French users. Or you're an international agency with a French client.
Someone on Slack asks: « What's the RGAA? Is that just WCAG with a French name? »
Not quite.
The RGAA (Référentiel Général d'Amélioration de l'Accessibilité) is France's legally binding accessibility standard. It's based on WCAG 2.1, but it's stricter, more prescriptive, and legally enforceable in France via the Défenseur des droits (Ombudsman) and, since June 2025, on private sector websites too.
If your French website isn't RGAA-compliant, you're exposed to legal complaints, public pressure, and—if you're large enough—pressure from the French government.
I've been working on accessibility for ten years. I've tested hundreds of websites across Europe. French websites in particular often miss these standards. Not malice. Just lack of clear resources in English.
This article is for teams who don't speak French, manage French users, and need to understand what's actually required.
WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) is the international standard from the W3C. It has three levels: A, AA, AAA. It covers contrast, keyboard navigation, alternative text for images, captions for video, semantic HTML—all the things that make a website usable for people with disabilities.
RGAA is a French translation and adaptation of WCAG 2.1, but with one key difference: it's prescriptive, not descriptive.
WCAG says: « Check the contrast ratio of your text. »
RGAA says: « Use axe DevTools or WAVE to measure contrast. The minimum is 4.5:1 for normal text. Here's exactly how to verify it. »
WCAG lea

zh.title: RGAA 4.1：为非法语团队详解法国网络无障碍标准
zh.summary: RGAA 4.1是法国基于WCAG 2.1制定的法律规定的网络无障碍标准，但更为严格和规范，自2025年6月起对私营部门网站强制执行。与WCAG的描述性指南不同，RGAA规定了具体的工具和验证方法，建立了与国际标准不同的合规要求。管理法国网站的团队必须满足RGAA标准，超越标准WCAG AA合规，以规避法律风险。

title: I'm Not a Community Person. I'm Starting a Community Project Anyway.
summary: A software engineer launched FluxHelp, a free community tech help initiative to bridge the gap between technology experts and non-technical users struggling with basic digital tasks. The project emphasizes teaching rather than just fixing, enabling users to solve future problems independently rather than repeatedly paying for service calls. This addresses a real access gap in affordable, judgment-free technical support in the community.
content:
I'm not a community person. I say that without shame. It's just how I'm wired. My energy is finite, my brain is protective of it, and most of my life has been spent in front of a screen solving problems nobody asked me to solve. I show up to things when I have to. I leave when I can. I've never been the person who joins committees or volunteers at events or posts in the neighborhood Facebook group.
So naturally, I'm starting a free tech help initiative in Springfield. Obviously.
I work in IT. I've been around technology my whole life. Building things, breaking things, figuring out how things work since I was twelve. I went to school for it. Got degrees in it. Taught classes in it. And the one constant across all of that is the gap between people who understand technology and people who need it to function in daily life.
That gap isn't about intelligence. It's about access. It's about having someone you can ask who won't make you feel stupid for asking.
I've watched people pay $200 to get a printer reconnected to WiFi. I've seen someone almost fall for a scam popup because they didn't know those weren't real warnings. I've talked to people who just need someone to sit with them for twenty minutes and explain where the setting is. But that twenty minutes doesn't exist in their world, because every option costs money or dignity or both.
FluxHelp is me offering what I already do for friends and family: tech help, no charge, no judgment, no "well actually."
But here's the part that matters to me. This isn't just "hand me your laptop and I'll fix it." I mean, I'll absolutely do that if that's what you need. But I'd rather teach you what's happening and why, so next time you can handle it yourself. Or at least know what questions to ask.
I actually taught college classes for a bit. C# and Android development. I liked the explaining part, but I'll be honest, it left me frustrated. Most of the students were there because someone told them tech jobs pay well. They weren't stu

zh.title: 我不是社区人士。但我还是在启动一个社区项目。
zh.summary: 一名软件工程师启动了FluxHelp，这是一个免费的社区技术帮助计划，旨在缩小技术专家与在基本数字任务上困难的非技术用户之间的差距。该项目强调教学而不仅仅是修复，使用户能够独立解决未来的问题，而不是反复支付技术支持费用。这解决了社区中负担得起、无评判的技术支持的可及性差距。
